channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Erdogan Has Laid a Trap in Ankara
item.description: No matter what happens at the NATO summit, the Turkish strongman wins.

item.title: If ludicrous Trump flattery can save NATO, bring it on
item.description: The NATO chief Mark Rutte is quite annoying. It is in a worthy cause

item.title: What to Expect at the 2026 NATO Summit
item.description: It’s a precarious time for the world’s largest military alliance.

item.title: The World Bank has ditched its climate targets
item.description: The rise and fall of the institution’s love for green growth

item.title: Beware the top-heavy economy
item.description: Supersize capital flows are reshaping business—and building risk

item.title: Are U.S.-India Ties Really Thawing?
item.description: As the two sides near a final trade deal, a few sticking points remain.

item.title: Why This Year’s NATO Summit Is Different for Ukraine
item.description: Ukraine’s ambassador to the United States breaks it down in a new interview.

item.title: Mexico is placing an enormous bet on rail
item.description: Can it keep its plans on track?

item.title: A rare Chinese missile test unnerves Asian neighbours
item.description: A nuclear submarine test-fired a ballistic missile into the Pacific

item.title: The world’s most, and least, liveable cities in 2026
item.description: See how 173 cities compare in EIU’s annual ranking

item.title: There Is Only One Acceptable Path for Designer Babies
item.description: Genetic-engineering tools with which to design one's progeny already exist, and they are being made even more potent with AI—at least for those who can afford such services. In the absence of policy interventions, we will be hurtling toward a tiered society in which economic privileges become biological advantages.

item.title: US Tariffs Won’t End Forced Labor, But They Can Change the Incentives That Drive It
item.description: Properly designed and tied to labor-based risk, U.S. tariffs could change the incentives that sustain labor abuse, especially in China.

item.title: Tashiev Convicted in Coup Plot, Granted Probation Instead of Prison
item.description: That Tashiev was given probation after a coup plot conviction while Kyrgyz journalists and musicians sit in jail, or exile, for reporting on his family’s corruption says everything that needs to be said about the character of justice and politics in Kyrgyzstan.

item.title: Promotions Herald a New Leadership for China’s Military
item.description: Amid an ongoing purge, Chinese leader Xi Jinping may be assembling the cornerstones of a new Central Military Commission.

item.title: The Democratic Turkey NATO Needs
item.description: At NATO's upcoming summit in Ankara, Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan will seek to portray his country as stable, confident, and essential to Western security. But only the last one is true, which is why the restoration of democracy in Turkey is more than a domestic concern.

item.title: China’s Moment to Shape a New Global Order
item.description: Recent visits to Beijing by Trump and Putin and a more favorable geopolitical environment have given Beijing more strategic space to advance its vision.

item.title: Britain’s Farms Run on Central Asian Labor Now
item.description: As Russia squeezes migrants, Central Asians now fill nearly four in five U.K. Seasonal Worker visas.

item.title: Twice Stateless: The Double Erasure of Bhutanese Refugees
item.description: They survived ethnic cleansing in Bhutan, decades living in remote camps in Nepal, and the grueling process of starting over in the United States – only to be deported.

item.title: Israel Belongs in the New Saudi-Iranian Order
item.description: An anti-Abraham Accords is taking shape. That would be a big mistake.

item.title: War Has Become Pointless
item.description: Military conflict has always existed. It’s just stopped making sense.

item.title: The World Cup as It Should Be
item.description: While Donald Trump was widely expected to lord over this year’s World Cup, which the United States is co-hosting with Mexico and Canada, it has turned out not to be his scene. National teams populated with immigrants, and small-town America’s embrace of the event, go against everything Trump’s MAGA movement stands for.

item.title: The U.S. Can’t ‘Yield Even an Inch’ to Xi, Top Democrat Says
item.description: Sen. Tammy Duckworth tells FP why she is traveling to Taiwan.

item.title: The Real Threat to NATO
item.description: The greatest challenge to NATO comes not from its adversaries but from within. As its leaders gather in Ankara on July 7-8, they should focus less on military capabilities than on reinvigorating the alliance's founding strategic philosophy, centered on democratic legitimacy, human rights, and the rule of law.

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Presidents and profits
item.description: Donald Trump’s legacy is still unfolding. “In the end he may be best known for reaping riches from the office only 45 men have had the honour to hold,” writes Charlotte Howard, our US editor

item.title: How the EU Can Lead the Rest of the West
item.description: While Europe is not just another middle power, nor is it a great power with sufficient economic, military, and geopolitical clout to dictate terms on the global stage. Instead, Europe is the ideal candidate to convene and lead a group of like-minded middle powers, not as a US-style hegemon, but as a first among equals.

item.title: Europe Must Face the World on Its Own
item.description: Under pressure from Russian aggression—which has reached its full expression in the ongoing war against Ukraine—and an increasingly hostile US administration, Europe urgently needs to make progress toward a defense union. But that will require Franco-German leadership of the kind that is not yet on offer.

item.title: Denying Great Power Interference: Vietnam’s Strategies Toward Laos and Cambodia
item.description: Since the first Vietnamese declaration of independence in 1945, Hanoi has pursued a consistent policy toward its two western neighbors.

item.title: Luv Puri on the Unrest Roiling Pakistan-administered Jammu and Kashmir
item.description: “The recurring protests point to accumulating grievances over governance, representation, and the relationship between the region and Pakistan's federal establishment.”

item.title: Why is the RSS, the Parent Organization of India’s Ruling BJP, Still Unregistered?
item.description: The century-old RSS has always maintained that it is a “body of individuals” that does not need to register and pay taxes.

item.title: Why India Cannot Escape Blame for Dhaka’s Handing Over of Key Projects to China
item.description: In 2015, Bangladesh allotted land to India for the development of an SEZ at Mongla port. India failed to start work on that project. Dhaka handed it to China.

item.title: Jimmy Carter Knew What Ails America
item.description: One of history’s most misunderstood speeches is also one of its most important.

item.title: A Better Way to Build AI
item.description: America’s dominance depends on getting local communities on board.

item.title: The Transatlantic Alliance Can’t Survive Without Trust
item.description: Washington dismisses NATO’s value at its own peril.

item.title: The Transatlantic Alliance Can’t Survive Without Trust
item.description: Washington dismisses NATO’s value at its own peril.

item.title: Women’s progress at work appears to be stalling
item.description: After the “lean in” generation, are professional women leaning out?
